questions.prompt: In the direct graphical method, a real root of f(x)=0 is seen where:
options: The graph reaches its maximum value
questions.explanation: A root is exactly a point where f(x)=0, so on the plot y=f(x) it appears where the curve crosses or touches the horizontal axis.

questions.prompt: Why does plotting 1/|f(x)| help reveal roots?
options: Because values near a root create large peaks
Because derivatives are no longer needed
questions.correctAnswer: Because values near a root create large peaks
questions.explanation: When f(x) approaches zero, the denominator |f(x)| becomes very small, so 1/|f(x)| becomes very large. That makes root locations stand out visually.

questions.prompt: A guaranteed bisection start requires which condition on [a,b]?
options: f(a)f(b)<0 and continuity on the interval
The midpoint must already be close to the root
questions.correctAnswer: f(a)f(b)<0 and continuity on the interval
questions.explanation: Bisection relies on the Intermediate Value Theorem. A continuous function with opposite signs at the endpoints must cross zero somewhere in the interval.

questions.prompt: The midpoint used in bisection is:
questions.explanation: Bisection literally halves the interval, so the candidate point is the midpoint x=(a+b)/2 before checking which half still contains the sign change.

questions.prompt: If f(xm) has the same sign as f(a), what is the correct bisection update?
options: Restart with a new random interval
questions.explanation: If f(xm) and f(a) share the same sign, the root cannot lie between a and xm. The valid sign change remains between xm and b, so the lower endpoint moves up.

questions.prompt: How does the interval width change after each bisection step?
options: It becomes the square of the previous width
questions.explanation: Each iteration selects one of the two half-intervals, so the new bracket length is exactly half of the previous one. That is the source of bisection's linear convergence.

questions.prompt: Which expression is the standard bisection error bound after k steps?
questions.explanation: Since the interval length shrinks by a factor of 2 each time and the midpoint lies at most half an interval away from the true root, the midpoint error is bounded by (b-a)/2^(k+1).

questions.prompt: What is the strongest advantage of bisection compared with Newton-Raphson?
options: It is guaranteed under the sign-change assumptions
It also finds all complex roots automatically
questions.correctAnswer: It is guaranteed under the sign-change assumptions
questions.explanation: Bisection is slower, but it is robust. If the function is continuous and the initial interval contains a sign change, the method cannot lose the root.

questions.prompt: Why is 1/|f(z)| useful in the complex plane?
options: It turns complex roots into visible peaks
questions.correctAnswer: It turns complex roots into visible peaks
questions.explanation: At complex roots, |f(z)| becomes small, so the reciprocal becomes large. Surface or contour plots then reveal the locations of complex zeros geometrically.

questions.prompt: Bisection is called a bracketing method because:
options: It keeps the root trapped inside an interval
It uses square brackets in MATLAB code
It only works for bracket polynomials
questions.correctAnswer: It keeps the root trapped inside an interval
questions.explanation: The whole idea is to maintain an interval whose endpoints keep opposite signs. That bracket surrounds the root at every step, which is why the method is so reliable.
